mentions.note: Dividend aristocrat (69 consecutive years) tumbled from $180; now 3% yield; new CEO catalyst; buy in tranches at 3%, 3.25%, 4% yields to build position.
Recession-hedge defensive play on credit card cap policy fear; stock rallying despite weak fundamentals signals market pricing in weak economy scenario.
Don't expect fireworks, but stock down so much it can bounce; near 3% yield attractive; holding for Charitable Trust.

Rallied 3% on weak Q4 quarter (flat organic sales vs. 5% expected, baby/feminine down 4%) due to crushed expectations and new CEO Shilesh Dejurikar credibility; international strength (Brazil +8%, China +3%) and innovation (284 new products, +68% YTD) suggest turnaround; stock bounced from $137 Jan 7 low to $150; Cramer bought for charitable trust at $146 and $141; more room to run.
Rallied on what many thought was bad quarter; provides positive precedent for similar companies.
Nearly half of sales overseas; weak dollar will boost competitiveness and earnings. Went up gigantically after soft quarter, positioning for margin recovery.
Big software spender benefiting from rotation to cost-savers; had bad quarter but stock way up; enterprise buyer winning in AI era.
Consumer packaged goods; tangible products; understable business; high demand; reports tomorrow.
February winner; defensive staples; beneficiary of risk-off rotation.
Caller asked if buy at 8% off 52-week high; 6% dividend increase. Cramer discussed with Matt Horwitz; bond market headwind will determine utility/dividend performance. Don't buy PG right now.
Friday earnings with new CEO; quarter expected weak, not yet time for turnaround; but stock cheap as hedge on slowdown; own for trust; lowest valuation in years.
Owned by Cramer's charitable trust; tempted to buy more but no catalyst for rebound near-term given rate environment.
Best-of-breed company with great brands, long dividend history, and proven balance sheet. Worth paying up for quality even if not on sale.

Core defensive holding in the charitable trust providing cushion during today's tech meltdown. Acts as a 'permit' enabling safe ownership of riskier growth plays. Doesn't boom but doesn't crash either.
Cited as the archetypal secular growth consumer staple — consistent earnings regardless of economic conditions; used as a teaching example, not a current pick.
